 {"id":"bd-2oav","title":"WAL-FEC: Pipelined background FEC job (durable vs repairable semantics)","description":"Implement the default required pipelining for repair symbol generation.

Spec rules (normative):
- Commit is durable once .wal frames are written+fsync and wal-index updated.
- Commit becomes repairable only after WalFecGroupMeta + R SymbolRecords are appended to .wal-fec and fsync.
- Encoding MUST NOT happen inside WAL write critical section.

Implementation:
- After commit durability, enqueue background job:
  - read source frames from .wal
  - deterministically generate repair symbols
  - append meta+symbols to .wal-fec
  - fsync .wal-fec

Optional:
- Synchronous mode MAY wait for .wal-fec fsync before ack commit; off by default.

Done when:
- concurrency/ordering is correct and tested.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:38:12.731973216Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-09T21:53:14.685933956Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T21:50:09.033701027Z","close_reason":"Pipelined background FEC job implemented via WalFecRepairPipeline in wal_fec.rs. Encoding outside WAL write critical section, background thread reads source frames, generates repair symbols, appends to .wal-fec, fsyncs. Async pipeline tests pass.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2oav","depends_on_id":"bd-1gyi","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:53:14.685886828Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2oav","depends_on_id":"bd-2ha1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:53:14.499840506Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":960,"issue_id":"bd-2oav","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Commit is durable once .wal frames written+fsync and wal-index updated.
- [ ] Commit becomes repairable after WalFecGroupMeta + R SymbolRecords appended to .wal-fec and fsync.
- [ ] FEC encoding does NOT happen inside WAL write critical section.
- [ ] Background job reads source frames, generates repair symbols, appends to .wal-fec, fsyncs.
- [ ] Ordering: commits become repairable in commit order.
- [ ] Optional synchronous mode waits for .wal-fec fsync before ack.
- [ ] Concurrency is correct under concurrent writers and readers.

## Unit Test Requirements
- test_pipeline_commit_durable_before_repairable: Commit visible before FEC completes.
- test_pipeline_encoding_outside_critical_section: WAL lock not held during encode.
- test_pipeline_ordering: Multiple commits become repairable in order.
- test_pipeline_sync_mode: Synchronous mode blocks until .wal-fec fsynced.
- test_pipeline_concurrent_writers: Two concurrent writers produce correct FEC.

## E2E Test
- test_e2e_fec_pipeline: Write multiple commits, verify repairable status progression.

## Logging Requirements
- DEBUG: emit job enqueue, encode start/end, and fsync timing.
- INFO: emit commit durable and repairable state transitions.
- WARN: emit pipeline backpressure or slow encode.
- ERROR: emit .wal-fec write failures with reproducible context.

Test insert_multi_row_values passes.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":1004,"issue_id":"bd-2of2","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Repro (current behavior):
- CREATE TABLE tm (v INTEGER);
- INSERT INTO tm VALUES (1), (2), (3);
- SELECT v FROM tm;
=> only inserts the first row.

Root cause hint: fsqlite-vdbe codegen_insert currently selects rows[0] with a "First row for now" comment.

Goal: loop over VALUES rows, emit record+insert per row, and ensure row_count/RETURNING semantics are correct.","created_at":"2026-02-10T02:34:03Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-2pcg","title":"Overhead: Implement PRAGMA raptorq_overhead (defaults, persistence, per-object policy)","description":"Implement `PRAGMA raptorq_overhead` as specified.

## Semantics
- `PRAGMA raptorq_overhead = <percent>` sets the default multiplicative overhead policy.
- The value is used by budgeting to derive `R` from `K_source`:
  - `R = max(slack_decode, ceil(K_source * overhead_percent / 100))` with `slack_decode=2`.

## Persistence / Scope
- Define whether the setting is:
  - connection-local (default SQLite style), and/or
  - persisted into database metadata for replication determinism.

If persisted, it MUST be versioned and included in any object metadata required for deterministic decoding/proofs.

## Interaction With Object Types
- Provide a mechanism to override overhead per object class (markers vs pages vs index segments), even if V1 exposes only a global PRAGMA.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Setting is parsed/validated (bounds, rounding rules) and stored deterministically.
- Budgeting API exposes the effective overhead used for each object (for evidence/proofs).
- Unit tests cover:
  - bounds clamping
  - rounding behavior
  - small-K behavior (no under-provisioning)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:53:34.855360204Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-09T22:08:50.790213962Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T22:08:50.790188815Z","close_reason":"Implemented PRAGMA raptorq_overhead policy state: deterministic parse/clamp/rounding, optional versioned persisted metadata scope, per-object overrides, and policy_controller unit coverage for bounds/rounding/small-K/exposure.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2pcg","depends_on_id":"bd-3lpo","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:54:38.549657004Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:21:39Z"},{"id":1122,"issue_id":"bd-2r4z","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Task: Build FEC sidecar generation utility (db-fec and wal-fec)

### What
Build the utility that generates RaptorQ erasure-coded sidecar files (.db-fec, .wal-fec) for a given database. These sidecars contain the repair symbols that enable corruption recovery.

### Background (§3.4.6 Erasure-Coded Page Storage)
- Database divided into page groups of G=64 pages (256KB)
- Each group encoded with R=4 repair symbols
- Repair symbols stored in `.db-fec` sidecar file
- Sidecar header includes `db_gen_digest` = BLAKE3(change_counter + page_count + freelist_count + schema_cookie)
- Per-source validation via xxh3_128 hash

### Design
```rust
pub struct FecGenerator {
    page_size: usize,
    group_size: usize,   // G = 64 (default)
    repair_count: usize, // R = 4 (default)
}

pub struct FecSidecar {
    pub db_gen_digest: [u8; 32],  // BLAKE3 of database identity
    pub groups: Vec<PageGroup>,
}

pub struct PageGroup {
    pub group_index: u32,
    pub source_pages: Vec<u32>,     // Page numbers in this group
    pub repair_symbols: Vec<Vec<u8>>,  // R repair symbols, each page_size bytes
    pub source_hashes: Vec<[u8; 16]>,  // xxh3_128 per source page
}

impl FecGenerator {
    /// Generate .db-fec sidecar for a database file
    pub fn generate_db_fec(&self, db_path: &Path) -> Result<FecSidecar> { ... }

    /// Generate .wal-fec sidecar for a WAL file
    pub fn generate_wal_fec(&self, wal_path: &Path) -> Result<WalFecSidecar> { ... }

    /// Attempt to repair a corrupted page using its group's FEC data
    pub fn repair_page(&self, sidecar: &FecSidecar, db_path: &Path, page_number: u32) -> Result<Vec<u8>> { ... }
}
```

### RaptorQ Integration
Uses the `fsqlite-raptorq` crate (already in workspace) for:
- `encode()`: Generate repair symbols from source pages
- `decode()`: Reconstruct corrupted pages from surviving sources + repairs
- `verify()`: Validate per-source xxh3_128 hashes

### Header Page Special Case (§3.4.6)
Page 1 (header) uses G=1, R=4 (400% redundancy). The header is so critical that losing it makes the entire database unreadable, so we store 4 repair copies.

### db_gen_digest Calculation
```rust
fn compute_db_gen_digest(db_path: &Path) -> [u8; 32] {
    let header = read_first_100_bytes(db_path);
    let change_counter = u32::from_be_bytes(header[24..28]);
    let page_count = u32::from_be_bytes(header[28..32]);
    let freelist_count = u32::from_be_bytes(header[36..40]);
    let schema_cookie = u32::from_be_bytes(header[40..44]);
    blake3::hash(&[change_counter, page_count, freelist_count, schema_cookie].concat_bytes())
}
```

### Acceptance Criteria
- .db-fec generation works for any SQLite database
- Repair successfully recovers a corrupted page when R > corrupted pages in group
- Per-source xxh3_128 validation detects corruption
- db_gen_digest rejects stale sidecars
- Header page has G=1, R=4 special handling
- Unit tests for encode → corrupt → decode cycle
","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:23:11Z"},{"id":1157,"issue_id":"bd-2r4z","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## REVISION NOTE (IvoryWaterfall audit)

### Issue: Dependency on fsqlite-raptorq API surface
This bead assumes fsqlite-raptorq provides `encode()` and `decode()` functions for RaptorQ encoding. Before implementing, verify:
1. `crates/fsqlite-raptorq/` exists and has these APIs
2. The API accepts page-sized chunks as source symbols
3. The API returns repair symbols of the same size
4. The decode function handles the "K symbols needed, K+R available" math

If the API doesn't match, either:
- Adapt the FEC generator to the existing API
- Or extend the raptorq crate with the needed functions (separate bead)

### Testing: Full encode→corrupt→decode roundtrip
The key test is the full cycle:
```rust
#[test]
fn test_fec_roundtrip() {
    let db = create_test_database(1000);  // 1000 rows, multiple pages
    let sidecar = FecGenerator::default().generate_db_fec(&db)?;

    // Corrupt one page
    let injector = CorruptionInjector::new(db.clone());
    injector.inject(&CorruptionPattern::PageZero { page_number: 5 })?;

    // Repair
    let repaired = FecGenerator::default().repair_page(&sidecar, &db, 5)?;
    let original = read_page(&golden_db, 5)?;
    assert_eq!(repaired, original, "Page not correctly repaired");
}
```

### Cross-Reference
- Related to (and supersedes) bd-1oha (RaptorQ Sidecar Encoder, CLOSED)
- Related to (and supersedes) bd-1q38 (RaptorQ Repair Tool, CLOSED)
- Both closed beads had less detail; this bead provides the comprehensive implementation spec.
","created_at":"2026-02-10T05:07:07Z"},{"id":1165,"issue_id":"bd-2r4z","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## bd-2r4z: FEC Sidecar Generation Utility — COMPLETE

### Implementation (crates/fsqlite-core/src/db_fec.rs)

Added the following public API to the existing db_fec module:

**Data structures:**
- `DbHeaderFields` — parsed SQLite header fields for FEC generation

**Generation functions:**
- `parse_db_header_fields(data)` — parse header from raw bytes
- `read_db_header_fields(path)` — parse header from file
- `generate_db_fec_from_bytes(db_data)` — generate complete .db-fec sidecar from raw database bytes
- `generate_db_fec_sidecar(db_path)` — generate sidecar from file path
- `write_db_fec_sidecar(db_path)` — generate and write .db-fec sidecar to disk

**Read/repair functions:**
- `read_db_fec_header(sidecar_path)` — read DbFecHeader from .db-fec file
- `read_db_fec_group_for_page(sidecar_data, header, target_pgno)` — read group meta + repair symbols for a page
- `db_fec_path_for_db(db_path)` — compute .db-fec sidecar path

### Sidecar Layout
`[DbFecHeader (52B)][Seg_page1][Seg_group0][Seg_group1]...`
- General segments padded to full_segment_len for O(1) random access
- Page 1: G=1, R=4 (400% redundancy)
- Other pages: G=64, R=4

### Acceptance Criteria Verification
1. .db-fec generation for any SQLite database — PASS
2. Repair recovers corrupted pages — PASS (3 tests)
3. Per-source xxh3_128 validation — PASS
4. db_gen_digest rejects stale sidecars — PASS
5. Header page G=1, R=4 — PASS
6. Unit tests for encode->corrupt->decode — PASS

### Test Results
- 41 db_fec tests (30 existing + 11 new), all passing
- 649 total fsqlite-core tests, all passing
- Clippy clean (pedantic + nursery)

### WAL-FEC Status
WAL-FEC generation already complete via generate_wal_fec_repair_symbols() in fsqlite-wal/src/wal_fec.rs.

NOTE: The original consolidated spec extract for this rollup lives in the bead comments.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T05:16:43.848758099Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-08T17:33:36.442548986Z","closed_at":"2026-02-08T06:39:55.916081468Z","close_reason":"DUPLICATE: Superseded by finer-grained bd-1gae (§14.4) + bd-28j2 (§14.5) + bd-jzjn (§14.6) + bd-3gz3 (§14.7)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2rcq","depends_on_id":"bd-2k41","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T05:17:11.598330489Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2rcq","depends_on_id":"bd-3c7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T06:09:43.886110975Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":27,"issue_id":"bd-2rcq","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## §14.4-14.7 R*-Tree + Session + ICU + Miscellaneous Extensions

### R*-Tree (§14.4, `fsqlite-ext-rtree`)
Efficient spatial indexing (Beckmann et al., SIGMOD 1990). SQLite uses R*-tree variant (not original Guttman 1984).

`CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE idx USING rtree(id, minX, maxX, minY, maxY)`. 1-5 dimensions (2-10 coordinate columns). 32-bit floats default; `rtree_i32` for integers.

**Queries:** Range queries (bounding box constraints). Custom geometry callbacks implementing `RtreeGeometry: Send+Sync` with `query_func(bbox) → Include|Exclude|PartiallyContained`. Tree descent prunes Exclude branches.

**Geopoly extension:** Built on R*-tree. geopoly_overlap, within, area, blob, json, svg, bbox, contains_point, group_bbox, regular, ccw, xform. Polygons: 4-byte header + pairs of f32 coordinates.

### Session (§14.5, `fsqlite-ext-session`)
Records changes as changesets/patchsets for cross-database application.

**Changeset format:** Per table: 'T' byte, column count, PK flags, table name. Per row: operation byte (INSERT=18, DELETE=9, UPDATE=23). DELETE: old values. INSERT: new values. UPDATE: old+new values (undefined for unchanged non-PK). Values: type byte (0=undefined, 1=integer, 2=real, 3=text, 4=blob, 5=null) + data (varint-length-prefixed for text/blob, 8B BE for int/real).

**Conflict resolution:** ConflictAction enum (OmitChange, Replace, Abort). ConflictType enum (Data, NotFound, Conflict, Constraint, ForeignKey).

**Patchsets:** More compact: omits old values for UPDATE (only new+PK). Cannot detect conflicts as precisely.

### ICU (§14.6, `fsqlite-ext-icu`)
Unicode-aware string operations.

**Collation:** `icu_load_collation('de_DE', 'german')` creates collation from ICU locale. Uses `ucol_strcoll`.
**Case folding:** icu_upper/icu_lower(X, LOCALE) — locale-aware (vs ASCII-only built-in).
**FTS tokenizer:** `tokenize='icu zh_CN'` for language-aware word breaking via `UBreakIterator`. Critical for CJK.

### Miscellaneous (§14.7, `fsqlite-ext-misc`)
**generate_series(START,STOP [,STEP]):** Virtual table. Columns: value, start, stop, step.
**dbstat:** B-tree page usage stats (name, path, pageno, pagetype, ncell, payload, unused, mx_payload). aggregate hidden column.
**dbpage:** Direct page read/write. `SELECT data FROM dbpage WHERE pgno=1`.
**csv:** Virtual table for CSV files. filename, header, columns options.
**decimal:** Arbitrary-precision. decimal(), decimal_add/sub/mul, decimal_sum, decimal_cmp. String representation avoids float precision loss.
**uuid:** uuid() v4, uuid_str(X) blob→string, uuid_blob(X) string→16-byte blob.
","created_at":"2026-02-08T05:16:43Z"},{"id":103,"issue_id":"bd-2rcq","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## §14.5.1-§14.5.3 Full Spec Extract + Test/Logging Requirements (Session Extension)

This comment exists because the earlier bead summary for Session covered the concepts but did not explicitly include the numbered sub-subsections §14.5.1..§14.5.3.

### Session (`fsqlite-ext-session`)

The Session extension records changes to a database and represents them
as changesets or patchsets that can be applied to other databases.

#### §14.5.1 Changeset Format

A changeset is a binary blob with the following layout:
```
For each modified table:
  'T' byte (0x54)
  Number of columns (varint)
  For each column: 0x00 (not part of PK) or 0x01 (part of PK)
  Table name (nul-terminated string)

  For each changed row:
    Operation byte: SQLITE_INSERT (18), SQLITE_DELETE (9), SQLITE_UPDATE (23)

    For DELETE:
      Old values: one value per column (serial-type encoded)

    For INSERT:
      New values: one value per column (serial-type encoded)

    For UPDATE:
      Old values: one per column (undefined for non-PK columns that didn't change)
      New values: one per column (undefined for columns that didn't change)
```

Each value is encoded as: a single type byte (0x00=undefined, 0x01=integer,
0x02=real, 0x03=text, 0x04=blob, 0x05=null) followed by the value data
(varint-length-prefixed for text and blob, 8-byte big-endian for integer
and real).

#### §14.5.2 Conflict Resolution

When applying a changeset, conflicts are resolved via a callback:
```rust
pub enum ConflictAction {
    OmitChange,     // skip this change
    Replace,        // overwrite conflicting row
    Abort,          // abort the entire apply operation
}

pub enum ConflictType {
    Data,           // row exists but values differ from expected
    NotFound,       // row to update/delete does not exist
    Conflict,       // unique constraint violation
    Constraint,     // other constraint violation
    ForeignKey,     // foreign key constraint
}
```

#### §14.5.3 Patchset Differences

A patchset is a more compact format that omits the old values for UPDATE
operations (only stores new values and PK). Patchsets cannot detect
conflicts as precisely as changesets (cannot verify that the old row matched)
but are significantly smaller for tables with many columns.

### Additional FrankenSQLite Requirements (Bead-local)

1. Unit tests MUST cover:
   - changeset encode/decode round-trip for INSERT/DELETE/UPDATE
   - correct handling of undefined old/new values in UPDATE rows
   - value encoding for each type byte, including NULL and BLOB lengths
   - conflict callback invocation for each ConflictType, with each ConflictAction
2. E2E tests MUST cover:
   - generate changeset from DB-A, apply to DB-B, then verify DB-A == DB-B for the affected tables
   - negative tests: apply changeset where DB-B has diverged to force Data/NotFound/Conflict/Constraint/ForeignKey
   - patchset vs changeset behavioral difference: changeset detects mismatch where patchset cannot
3. Logging MUST include:
   - per apply: table name, op counts by kind, and whether conflicts occurred
   - on conflict: ConflictType, chosen ConflictAction, and a compact diff of expected vs found values

 {"id":"bd-4xpo","title":"Enable e2e logical state hash assertions now that ORDER BY rowid works","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:38:27.439302847Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T17:49:11.015360868Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T17:49:11.015341853Z","close_reason":"Enabled logical state hash assertions now that ORDER BY rowid works; correctness_sequential_insert + correctness_mixed_dml assert hash.matched; tests pass","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-5ic3","title":"Metadata/manifest hardening: stable JSON schema + backwards-compatible versioning","description":"## Goal

Ensure `metadata/*.json` and `manifests/manifest.v1.json` are stable contracts we can build automation/UI on.

## Requirements

- JSON schema (`manifest.v1.schema.json`) stays in sync with actual manifest.
- Deterministic ordering:
  - entries sorted by db_id
  - table lists and PRAGMA lists sorted
- Versioning:
  - explicit `manifest_version`
  - ability to introduce v2 without breaking tooling

## Acceptance

- A test enforces: schema validates manifest, and manifest+checksums+metadata are mutually consistent.
- Any future field additions are additive and do not break old readers.

(We still do not care about external backwards compatibility; this is about our own tooling stability.)","acceptance_criteria":"Acceptance Criteria

1. Manifest schema stability:
   - `sample_sqlite_db_files/manifests/manifest.v1.schema.json` validates `manifest.v1.json` in tests.
   - Manifest includes an explicit `manifest_version` (v1) and is designed so v2 can be introduced without breaking v1 tooling.

2. Deterministic generation:
   - Manifest entries are sorted by `db_id`.
   - Nested lists (tables, pragma summaries) use a deterministic order.

3. Cross-artifact consistency is enforced:
   - Every manifest entry has a corresponding `metadata/<db_id>.json` and checksum entry.
   - Every checksum entry refers to an existing golden file.

4. Regression tests:
   - CI test fails with a clear message if schema/manifest mismatch or ordering drift occurs.","notes":"Notes

- This is about *our internal tooling contract*, not external backwards compatibility.

","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:20:09Z"},{"id":1067,"issue_id":"bd-6d9v","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Task: Benchmark — large transaction (100K/1M row single-txn insert)

### What
Measure performance of very large transactions that stress the WAL and page cache.

### Benchmark Design
- Single transaction: BEGIN, INSERT N rows, COMMIT
- N = 100,000 and 1,000,000
- Table with 5 columns: id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, email TEXT, score REAL, created TEXT
- Row size ~200 bytes → 100K rows ≈ 20MB, 1M rows ≈ 200MB

### Why This Matters
Large transactions exercise:
- WAL growth (C SQLite: WAL file grows until checkpoint)
- MVCC version chain (FrankenSQLite: page versions accumulate)
- Memory pressure (page cache turnover)
- Commit latency (fsync of large WAL vs MVCC commit)

### Metrics
- Total time for INSERT phase
- Commit latency (time from last INSERT to COMMIT completion)
- Peak memory usage during transaction
- WAL file size at commit time (C SQLite) / version chain depth (FrankenSQLite)
- Post-VACUUM database size (should be identical)

### Expected Behavior
- FrankenSQLite may have advantages in commit speed (no WAL checkpoint needed)
- C SQLite may have advantages in insert speed (mature, optimized C code)
- Memory characteristics will differ due to MVCC vs WAL approach

### Acceptance Criteria
- Benchmarks for both 100K and 1M row transactions
- Total time, commit latency, and memory reported
- Both backends produce same final state (logical comparison)
- No OOM or crash on 1M row transaction
","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:20:43Z"}]}
